figures.caption: Fig. 1. UMV executing a front-flip learned via Iterative Motion Imitation.
Fig. 8. Overlay of UMV flipping up a 70 cm box.

Fig. 7. Three hardware flip experiments of UMV. The time is aligned at the peak height (t = 0) and the interval is ±0.5 s around that peak. (a) bike-base X-Z position, (b) lower-body and upper-body joint angles, (c) bike-base vertical velocity, and (d) bike-base vertical acceleration. The vertical dashed line indicates the time at take-off.
Fig. 2. A 2D overview of the UMV model used in this work.

Fig. 3. An overview of IMI. The first iteration starts with an initial reference ξ0 used to train policy π1. Then, reference ξn−1 generated by policy πn−1 becomes the new more feasible reference used to train a new policy πn. This loop continues, progressively refining the motion until a high-performance, hardware-deployable policy is achieved.
TABLE I REWARD STRUCTURE WITH TOLERANCE
Fig. 4. Overlayed screenshots of UMV performing a front flip.
Fig. 5. Comparison of training from scratch (ξ0) versus using refined reference (ξ1). Each run uses 3 seeds. The solid line is the mean and the shaded area is the standard deviation.

Fig. 6. The evolution of trajectories ξ over two iterations. The time is aligned at the peak height (t = 0) and the interval is ±0.5 s around that peak. (a) bike-base X-Z position, (b) lower-body and upper-body joint angles, (c) bike-base vertical velocity, and (d) bike-base vertical acceleration.
